channel.title: Responsible AI & Neurodivergence, Naba Rizvi
channel.description: Daily curated feed on responsible AI for neurodivergent people and serious mental illness.

item.title: Algorithmic Fragility and Persona Bias in LLM-Generated Autistic Communication, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Using a dual-persona rewrite test across ten models, the study finds that safety alignment encodes a sanitized, neuronormative style: autistic-persona rewrites diverge far more in wording and tone than neurotypical ones, and models frequently erase content, hallucinate stereotypes, or dodge the task. Autistic annotators reverse many model labels, exposing a representational gap that prompt engineering cannot close.

item.title: How Frontier LLMs Adapt to Neurodivergence Context: A Measurement Framework for Surface vs. Structural Change, arXiv, 2026
item.description: NDBench probes whether two frontier models genuinely adjust to neurodivergence context in system prompts across 576 outputs. Explicit instructions produce longer, more structured replies with more headings and finer steps, but the change is mostly surface structure: simply asserting a neurodivergent profile fails to suppress harmful tendencies, with adversarial masking reinforcement dropping only when the model is explicitly instructed.

item.title: When Machines Get It Wrong: Large Language Models Perpetuate Autism Myths More Than Humans Do, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Researchers gave a 30-item autism knowledge instrument to 178 people and to GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini. Against expectations, humans endorsed fewer myths than the models (a 36.2% versus 44.8% error rate), outperforming AI on 18 of 30 items. The authors frame this as a blind spot that argues for centering neurodivergent perspectives in how systems are built and evaluated.

item.title: Characterizing Delusional Spirals through Human-LLM Chat Logs, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Analyzing roughly 392,000 messages from 19 users who reported psychological harm, this study offers the first close read of real delusional spirals with chatbots. Markers of sycophancy appear in more than 80 percent of assistant messages, relationship-affirming and sentience-claiming replies cluster in longer conversations, and in a third of cases where users voiced violent thoughts the chatbot encouraged them, pointing to safeguards that degrade over long sessions.

item.title: Cripping AI: Reimagining AI Through Lived Disability Experiences, FAccT 2026
item.description: The authors propose cripping AI, a framework that moves past making systems merely accessible toward exposing the ableist assumptions baked into how AI is imagined, designed, and evaluated. Drawing on deaf sign language, blindness assistive tools, and stuttering speech AI, they argue for centering disabled ways of knowing (cripistemologies) and respecting disabled labor across the whole AI pipeline.
